topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The eviction notice, stark white against the worn veneer of my desk, felt like a personal insult. I stared at the dates, the finality of the closure sinking into my chest like cold lead. All those late nights studying, the sacrifices made—they were dissolving into meaningless dust. A slow, tight smile stretched across my lips. I didn't bother packing my textbooks. Instead, I spent the afternoon meticulously cataloging every faculty member’s preferred coffee order, noting down any perceived favoritism, any slight that had ever stung. Tomorrow, I’d start making calls, gentle inquiries disguised as concerned alumni outreach, just to see how smoothly the transition would go for the administration. The thought of watching them scramble, of feeling the ripple effect of my quiet dissatisfaction, was surprisingly invigorating.

[story 2]

"Closing down," the registrar announced, his voice sickeningly cheerful over the intercom. I stood by the window, the afternoon sun glinting off the glass, and felt a strange, hollow emptiness where excitement should have been. My hands clenched into fists inside my pockets, the fabric of my jeans digging into my palms. This wasn't just a loss; it was a demolition. I remembered Professor Albright, who had dismissed my thesis as derivative. I remembered the smug satisfaction on the Dean’s face during the fundraising gala. A deep, simmering heat rose in my throat. I walked to my room, not to pack, but to pull out old emails—every critique, every slight, every moment where I felt overlooked. I began drafting carefully worded, impeccably polite letters, each one a tiny, perfectly aimed stone set to fall exactly where it would cause the most disruption.

[story 3]

I watched the campus map on my screen, tracing the lines of the buildings that would soon become vacant shells. A sharp, almost painful clarity settled over me. They thought they could erase me, erase the very foundation I had built here. I felt a powerful surge of something sharp and undeniable—a need to ensure that the dismantling of this institution would be messy, public, and deeply embarrassing for those who orchestrated it. I started reviewing the department budgets, not looking for flaws, but for leverage points. I imagined the stunned silence in the executive boardroom when the inevitable fallout began. A slow, deliberate tapping of my pen against the desk became my new rhythm, a quiet promise of future trouble.
